prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, gather 'round, ye scurvy dog, and let ol' Captain tell ye the truth o' the heavens above!

Ye see, the sun — that blazin' goldpiece nailed to the firmament — casts down light that seems plain white to the eye, but 'tis a trickster, aye! That light be a treasure chest full o' colors, each one a different doubloon, shinin' at its own length o' wave, as the natural philosophers do claim.

Now, the sky above be not empty, no sir! 'Tis thick with tiny bits o' air — wee scallywags o' gas too small for any spyglass to spot. When the sun's light comes racin' down through that vast blue ocean o' sky, them little beggars grab hold o' the light and fling it about every which way, like a drunken crew tossin' coins in a tavern brawl.

But here be the cunning part, matey — them tiny sky-sprites favor the blue light most of all! The blue, bein' short o' wave and quick o' step, gets scattered hither and yon across the whole dome o' heaven, while the reds and yellows sail on straight and true, mindin' their own course.

So when ye cast yer weathered eye upward, ye be seein' that blue light bouncin' at ye from every corner o' the sky, like the flash o' sapphires spilled 'cross the deck.

And that, ye barnacle-brained bilge rat, be why the sky be blue! Now fetch me rum afore I keelhaul ye!
